answer: You can enable assigning a primary IPv6 address if you’re launching an instance into a dual-stack or IPv6-only subnet. Once you enable an IPv6 GUA address to be a primary IPv6, you cannot disable it. When you enable an IPv6 GUA address to be a primary IPv6, the first IPv6 GUA will be made the primary IPv6 address until the instance is terminated or the network interface is detached.

AWS Nitro Enclaves and hibernation cannot be enabled together on the same instance. The documentation references explaining their prerequisites are the [AWS Nitro Enclaves User Guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/enclaves/latest/user/) and the [hibernation prerequisites](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/hibernating-prerequisites.html).

The default is `standard` for T2 instances and `unlimited` for T3/T3a/T4g instances. For T3 instances with `host` tenancy, only `standard` is supported. To change this attribute after launch, use ModifyInstanceCreditSpecification.

The maximum number of instances you can launch in a single RunInstances request is the quota for the specified instance type for your account for the Region. If you specify a value that is more capacity than Amazon EC2 can launch in the target Availability Zone, Amazon EC2 launches the largest possible number of instances above the specified minimum count.

If you don't specify a subnet ID, AWS chooses a default subnet from your default VPC for you. If you don't have a default VPC, you must specify a subnet ID in the request. If you don't specify a security group ID, AWS uses the default security group for the VPC. If you don't specify a private IPv4 address, AWS chooses one from the IPv4 range of your subnet. These defaults do not apply if you do not have a default VPC (in which case you must specify a subnet ID).
